items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.

items.q: An atom is shown as mass number 40, atomic number 18. How many neutrons does it have?
items.why: Correct answer: 22. “18” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An atom has electron configuration 2,8,7. Which group is it in?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Group 7. “Group 2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many neutrons are in an atom of potassium-39 (atomic number 19)?
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
